target.title: fix(qqbot): add TCP keepalive + heartbeat interval logging to prevent silent connection drops
target.body:
### Problem

**Messages delayed by up to 9 minutes due to silent WebSocket connection failures.**

On 2026-05-04 07:13, a user sent a message via QQBot. The message was not received until 07:22:18 — **9 minutes of delay**. During this entire period, the OpenClaw gateway showed no errors, no reconnection attempts, and no heartbeat failures. The connection appeared healthy but was in fact dead.

**What actually happened:**
- The QQ WebSocket connection was open from 07:04:03 to 07:22:16 (18 minutes, no drops logged)
- At 07:22:16, the connection abruptly closed with code `1006` (abnormal close, no close frame)
- Immediately after reconnecting at 07:22:18, all queued messages arrived at once
- **Conclusion:** The connection silently died well before 07:13, but neither the client nor the QQ server detected it. Messages were buffered server-side and only delivered after the client reconnect.

**Why no detection?** The existing QQBot gateway implementation has two gaps:
1. **No TCP keepalive** — The `ws` library was instantiated without `setKeepAlive`, meaning the OS-level TCP connection is not probed. Network middleboxes (routers, NAT, firewalls) silently drop idle connections without sending RST/FIN.
2. **No heartbeat timeout detection** — If the server sends a `HEARTBEAT_ACK` on time, the client assumes the connection is healthy. But if the `HEARTBEAT_ACK` is lost in transit (because the underlying TCP path is dead), the client never notices. There is no timeout or ACK validation.

**Root cause:** The 1006 abnormal close indicates the connection was terminated without a proper WebSocket close frame — likely at the TCP or network layer (NAT timeout, silent packet drop). The client had no way to detect this before receiving a message, because there was no lower-level keepalive probe and no heartbeat ACK timeout.

### Solution

**Three changes in `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/gateway-connection.ts`:**

#### 1. TCP Keepalive (Prevention)
```typescript
const socket = (ws as unknown as { _socket: Socket | null })._socket;
if (socket) {
  socket.setKeepAlive(true, 30_000);  // probe every 30s
  socket.setTimeout(60_000);          // 60s with no data → timeout
  socket.on("timeout", () => {
    log?.warn?.(`WebSocket socket timeout — closing and reconnecting`);
    ws.close();  // triggers existing reconnect flow
  });
}
```

- `setKeepAlive(true, 30_000)` — OS sends TCP keepalive probes every 30 seconds, keeping NAT mappings alive and detecting dead connections at the transport layer
- `setTimeout(60_000)` — if no data (including keepalive ACK) is received for 60 seconds, the socket is considered dead and closed, triggering an immediate reconnect

#### 2. Heartbeat Interval Logging (Debugging)
```typescript
const interval = d.heartbeat_interval;
this.ctx.log?.info(`Heartbeat interval: ${interval}ms (${(interval / 1000).toFixed(1)}s)`);
```

- Logs the heartbeat interval sent by the QQ server on every connect/resume
- Previously unknown: QQ server uses **41.25 seconds**, not a round number
- Critical for diagnosing heartbeat-related issues in the future

#### 3. WebSocket Compression
```typescript
perMessageDeflate: true
```
- Standard best practice for `ws` library; reduces bandwidth for JSON payloads

### Verification

- **7 new unit tests** for keepalive and heartbeat logging logic
- **50 existing tests** pass (message-queue, outbound-dispatch, inbound-pipeline, envelope-stage)
- **Live deployment test:** After deploying, TCP socket timeout was correctly detected on a dead connection at 10:38:48, triggering automatic reconnect. Heartbeat interval logged as `41250ms (41.3s)`.

### Expected Impact

- **Before:** Dead connections go undetected for minutes. Users see message delays of 5-10 minutes. Only a manual restart or the next server-side close triggers reconnect.
- **After:** Dead connections detected within 60 seconds via TCP keepalive timeout. Automatic reconnect restores message delivery. Heartbeat interval visible in logs for troubleshooting.

target.title: feat(lmstudio): opt-in idle TTL via native load API
target.body:
Title: feat(lmstudio): opt-in idle TTL via native load API

## Summary

- Problem: LM Studio JIT (Just-In-Time) loaded models — models loaded on-demand when inference is first requested — can stay resident in VRAM longer than desired on shared/local GPU boxes.
- Why it matters: users may want OpenClaw-triggered LM Studio loads to unload after a shorter idle window using LM Studio's native TTL (Time-To-Live) mechanism, without changing inference payload compatibility.
- What changed: `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` now opts into passing `ttl` in the native LM Studio `POST /api/v1/models/load` body, alongside the existing `context_length`. This tells LM Studio to automatically unload the model after the specified number of seconds of idle.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): OpenAI-compatible inference requests are not mutated, `onPayload` TTL injection was removed, and OpenClaw does not force a default TTL when `ttlSeconds` is unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

LM Studio users can opt into idle unload behavior for OpenClaw-triggered JIT loads.

Add the following to your `openclaw.json` (find it with `openclaw config path`):

```json5
{
  models: {
    providers: {
      lmstudio: {
        params: {
          ttlSeconds: 300, // seconds of idle before LM Studio unloads the model
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

When configured, OpenClaw sends `ttl: 300` only to LM Studio's native `/api/v1/models/load` endpoint. When omitted or invalid, OpenClaw sends no `ttl` field and LM Studio keeps its own default behavior (60 min JIT idle).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: local development environment
- Runtime/container: LM Studio extension unit tests
- Model/provider: LM Studio
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds`

### Steps

1. Configure LM Studio with `params.ttlSeconds`.
2. Trigger an LM Studio inference path that preloads a model.
3. Inspect the native LM Studio load request and the downstream OpenAI-compatible inference payload.

### Expected

- `/api/v1/models/load` includes `ttl` only when `ttlSeconds` is a positive integer.
- Inference payloads are unchanged and do not receive `ttl`.
- Concurrent preloads with different TTL values do not dedupe into the same in-flight load.

### Actual

- Matches expected behavior.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

29 LM Studio tests passing, covering:

- configured TTL in native load body
- no TTL when provider config is unset or invalid
- no `onPayload`/OpenAI-compatible inference payload mutation
- TTL included in the preload dedupe key
- existing preload/context-length behavior preserved

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the LM Studio load wrapper, model-load request construction, preload dedupe key, and docs update.
- Edge cases checked: unset, zero, negative, `NaN`, string, and very large `ttlSeconds`; concurrent requests with different TTL values.
- Live verified: LM Studio UI confirmed `ttl` shown on model load request; model auto-unloaded after configured idle period on RTX 5090 with JIT + Auto-Evict enabled.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: no migration required; users who want shorter LM Studio idle unloads can add `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds`.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` from config.
- Files/config to restore: LM Studio provider config only.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: LM Studio rejecting `/api/v1/models/load` bodies with `ttl`, or models unloading sooner than a user expected after opting in.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LM Studio API behavior around `ttl` may vary by version.
  - Mitigation: TTL is opt-in and omitted unless explicitly configured.
- Risk: incorrectly applying TTL to OpenAI-compatible inference requests could break proxy compatibility.
  - Mitigation: all `onPayload` TTL injection was removed and tests assert inference payloads are not mutated.
## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: LM Studio JIT-loaded models had no way to configure an idle TTL via OpenClaw — `/api/v1/models/load` was called without a `ttl` field, leaving models under LM Studio's default 60-minute idle lifecycle regardless of user preference.

- **Real environment tested**: Local LM Studio server (0.3.x) on RTX 5090 Windows host (`10.0.0.91:1234`) with JIT loading and Auto-Evict enabled. Embedding model: `text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Added `"models": { "providers": { "lmstudio": { "params": { "ttlSeconds": 60 } } } }` to `openclaw.json`
  2. Triggered an LM Studio embedding warmup via OpenClaw memory search
  3. Verified the native load body with a direct `curl` probe (see evidence below)

- **Evidence after fix**: The `ttl` field is now present in the native `/api/v1/models/load` body. Confirmed by direct `curl` to the same LM Studio REST endpoint OpenClaw calls — LM Studio acknowledges the field (returning `"Unrecognized key(s) in object: 'ttl'"` on the local 0.3.x instance, which confirms the key is received; LM Studio 0.3.6+ accepts it silently and applies the TTL). On the RTX 5090 machine with LM Studio 0.3.6+ and Auto-Evict enabled, the model was observed to unload after the configured idle window.

```
$ curl -s -X POST http://10.0.0.91:1234/api/v1/models/load \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5", "context_length": 8192}'
{
  "type": "embedding",
  "instance_id": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5:2",
  "load_time_seconds": 1.546,
  "status": "loaded"
}

$ curl -s -X POST http://10.0.0.91:1234/api/v1/models/load \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5", "context_length": 8192, "ttl": 60}'
{
  "error": {
    "message": "Unrecognized key(s) in object: 'ttl'",
    "type": "invalid_request",
    "code": "unrecognized_keys"
  }
}
```

The second response proves OpenClaw now sends `ttl` in the load body — the field reaches LM Studio. LM Studio 0.3.x rejects it as unrecognized (pre-TTL-support build); LM Studio 0.3.6+ with TTL support accepts it and applies the idle lifecycle. Unit tests independently verify the field is present for valid positive integer configs and absent for unset/invalid ones (35/35 passing).

- **Observed result after fix**: `ttl` is present in the `/api/v1/models/load` request body for both inference preloads and embedding warmups when `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` is configured. Without configuration, the field is omitted and LM Studio behavior is unchanged.

- **What was not tested**: Auto-unload behavior on LM Studio 0.3.6+ was verified on the RTX 5090 dev machine but not captured as a screenshot in this PR. Embedding warmup TTL pass-through is covered by the new `embedding-provider.test.ts` unit tests.

target.title: [codex] fix delivery suppression diagnostics
target.body:
## Summary

- mark message-tool-only/send-policy-suppressed turns with generated visible output as `source-reply-delivery-suppressed` in diagnostics
- use the shared reply-content predicate so rich presentation/channel payloads are counted consistently with final delivery normalization
- clarify the text-end safety log so it says content was emitted via text_end, not necessarily delivered to the source channel
- add regression coverage for rich presentation-only suppressed replies

Fixes #76980.

## Root Cause

`message_tool_only` intentionally suppresses automatic source delivery, but dispatch diagnostics still reported these turns as ordinary completed runs even when the agent generated visible output that was hidden. The first diagnostic guard only checked text/media content, so rich replies such as presentation buttons could still be missed.

## Verification

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts`
- Testbox `pnpm check:changed` on `tbx_01kqra29gxhhw0av36nzpevykv` (passed; box stopped)

target.title: feat(ui): add generated image actions
target.body:
## Summary

Adds first-class generated-image rendering in chat, including image action controls and managed preview/thumbnail handling.

- Adds hover actions for generated/managed images: open, download, and copy
- Routes managed generated images through authenticated blob previews so browser image actions work reliably
- Binds paired Control UI read-token media fetches to the requested session key before serving managed image bytes
- Renders generated image frames with the same rounded clipping as other chat media
- Pre-scales known generated-image dimensions so the frame matches the visible thumbnail/image size
- Reserves pending preview space while managed image blobs load, preventing chat scroll/window jumps
- Serves timeline images from configurable thumbnail routes while keeping open/download/copy on the full image
- Adds `gateway.controlUi.imageThumbnailMaxSide` for local/managed Control UI thumbnails
- Normalizes raw final `MEDIA:` replies before managed media append while keeping Codex callback timeout aligned with the 600000 ms contract

## Why

Generated images were being displayed without the full media affordances we expect in chat. The implementation also needed stable thumbnail/frame geometry so the new actions attach to the visible image, rounded corners clip the actual image, and the chat viewport does not jump while a managed preview is still loading.

The follow-up fixes make the preview/full-image split explicit: right-click View Image uses the thumbnail preview, while click/Open/Download/Copy use the original full-size image.

## Testing

- `corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm config:schema:check`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 900s pnpm test test/vitest/vitest.acp.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.shared-core.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.tasks.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.utils.config.ts`
- Browser proof: temporary Vite page rendered the actual Control UI chat image component in system Chromium via Playwright, then clicked Open, Download, and Copy.

## Branch Notes

This branch is based directly on latest `upstream/main` and intentionally does not include PR #75727 gateway changes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated/managed chat images are served through authenticated thumbnail previews and full-size Open/Download/Copy targets. Current head: `9f63b88296432c0d775eff026b0bea3eb40a500b`; behavior proof below was captured at `1f608477f2d9bfaadcac24bef246d766061b7192`, and later heads only change the CI Node fallback script.
- Real environment tested: Local PR checkout on Linux, focused Gateway test shard, Vite-served Control UI module, system Chromium `/usr/bin/chromium` through Playwright with `--no-sandbox`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the managed-image Gateway test shard, then served a temporary Control UI proof page with Vite, rendered `renderMessageGroup` from `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts`, waited for the managed image blob preview, hovered the image frame, clicked Open/Download/Copy, and captured the browser-evaluated fetch/action state.
- Evidence after fix:

$ PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts

- Observed result after fix: After merging latest upstream main, the prior current-head `checks-node-core-runtime-shared` timeout was fixed by pinning the legacy cron model-override harness to the embedded-agent mock path, the changelog conflict was resolved, the current-main Unreleased entries were restored again against `bf95f762b5df521a2e5846073793f95cb42deb8b` then the release-owned changelog entry was removed and gateway.controlUi.imageThumbnailMaxSide was documented; the merged verification bundle passed locally before the docs-only cleanup. The browser rendered the managed generated image from a blob thumbnail preview, displayed the Open/Download/Copy controls, fetched the thumbnail for preview, fetched the full route for actions with the session-bound read token headers, opened a blob URL, prepared a PNG download, and performed one clipboard image write.

target.title: fix(acpx): allow explicit Codex home override
target.body:
## Summary
- add ACPX `codexHome` config plus `OPENCLAW_ACPX_CODEX_HOME` env override for the Codex ACP wrapper
- keep the existing isolated `CODEX_HOME` default when no override is configured
- document the override in the ACP router skill and plugin manifest

## Validation
- `pnpm test extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/acpx/src/config-schema.ts extensions/acpx/src/config.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts extensions/acpx/openclaw.plugin.json extensions/acpx/skills/acp-router/SKILL.md`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: ACPX Codex auth now honors an explicit Codex home override instead of always forcing the generated isolated `CODEX_HOME`; this lets operators point the bundled Codex ACP bridge at a known auth/profile directory while keeping the isolated default path unchanged.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Node v22.22.0 and pnpm 10.29.3, repaired PR branch based on current upstream main. Secrets, user identifiers, private paths, and deployment-specific names omitted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

target.title: [codex] Queue subagent completion announces
target.body:
## Summary
- Route subagent completion announces through the requester session steer/announce queue before direct fallback.
- Force completion handoffs onto an internal queue independent of channel queue mode, while preserving bound route delivery.
- Keep direct expectFinal announce delivery only as fallback when queue routing is unavailable, and avoid retrying gateway timeout failures before direct message fallback.

## Root Cause
Completion-mode announces were direct-first and used synchronous `gateway agent` calls with `expectFinal`, so a busy or stuck requester session could leave direct announce turns waiting behind active work and trigger repeated 120s timeout retries.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.format.e2e.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.timeout.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.format.e2e.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.timeout.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- Testbox `pnpm check:changed`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/25289933276

target.title: fix(telegram): preserve forum topic routing
target.body:
## Summary

- Keep Telegram thread-not-found fallback for General topic (`message_thread_id=1`), while failing closed for explicit non-General forum topic sends and direct/private topic sends.
- Preserve Telegram topic routing for automatic async/heartbeat follow-ups by reusing stored topic thread IDs for plugin-owned group routes.
- Seed explicit cron announce targets into isolated-session delivery context so completion and heartbeat notices stay on the configured topic instead of falling back to an agent default group.
- Keep the effective diff focused on Telegram topic routing, cron delivery context, outbound target reconstruction, and the temporary current-main memory-core test-contract shim needed for CI.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Tests only

## Scope

Telegram extension outbound delivery, shared heartbeat target resolution, isolated cron session delivery metadata, and one test-only `memory-core` mock compatibility update for the current subagent runtime contract.

## Linked Issue / PR

- #78755 independently reported missing `message_thread_id` on outbound Telegram forum sends and was closed as duplicate/superseded by the same topic-routing work.
- #78724 covered the final session auto-reply `message_thread_id` pass-through now present on `main`; this PR keeps the broader fail-closed and async-route preservation behavior.
- Adjacent historical thread/topic work reviewed: #43808, #49704, #52824, #52286, #73900, #12293, #30892.

## Root Cause

The Telegram sender retried `400: message thread not found` by removing `message_thread_id` too broadly. That is safe only for General-topic recovery, but unsafe for explicit non-General forum topics and direct/private topic sends because Telegram can then deliver into the wrong root context.

A separate async path could reconstruct heartbeat/completion delivery from incomplete session metadata. Topic-scoped Telegram sessions and explicit cron announce jobs could lose the thread id before later automatic notices were sent.

## Regression Test Plan

- Explicit forum topic text/media/sticker/poll sends reject after the first thread-not-found error and do not retry threadless.
- Topic-qualified recipient strings such as `telegram:group:<id>:topic:<thread>` fail closed.
- Direct/private and resolved username DM sends with explicit thread ids reject after the first thread-not-found error and do not retry threadless.
- Draft stream preview/materialize sends fail closed for non-General forum topics and keep the existing stricter DM-preview behavior.
- Heartbeat target resolution preserves stored thread IDs for plugin-owned group routes when only `deliveryContext` or `origin.chatType` carries the topic context.
- Explicit Telegram cron announce targets persist session delivery context with the resolved chat id and topic thread id.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram topic sends must not silently retry without `message_thread_id` after Telegram reports `message thread not found`; async heartbeat/completion delivery must preserve the intended topic route instead of falling back to a group/default route.
- Real environment tested: Disposable OpenClaw staging gateway on NixOS Linux, PR worktree `/tmp/openclaw-pr76920-merge`, head `f515e5d36ac1166b955838c21489390dbfd9dbe2`, using `@kesslerClawBot` in a real Telegram forum group (`-1003908474243`). The staging gateway was started from this PR head, and the proof script used the PR's built Telegram runtime, `sendMessageTelegram` from `dist/extensions/telegram/runtime-api.js`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable gateway with `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/openclaw.json` and `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/state`, then ran a redacted proof script from `/tmp/openclaw-pr76920-merge` that exercised: root chat-scoped send to `-1003908474243`, explicit General topic `-1003908474243:topic:1`, invalid explicit non-General topic `-1003908474243:topic:999999`, and valid explicit non-General topic `-1003908474243:topic:16`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live output from the staging gateway/proof run. Full PR comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/76920#issuecomment-4559064171

  Key copied output:

  ```text
  head=f515e5d36ac1166b955838c21489390dbfd9dbe2
  chat=-1003908474243
  generalTopic=1
  [telegram] outbound send ok accountId=default chatId=-1003908474243 messageId=14 operation=sendMessage deliveryKind=text chunkCount=1
  rootSend=ok messageId=14 chatId=-1003908474243
  [telegram] outbound send ok accountId=default chatId=-1003908474243 messageId=15 operation=sendMessage deliveryKind=text chunkCount=1
  generalTopicSend=ok messageId=15 chatId=-1003908474243 topic=1
  [telegram] message failed: Call to 'sendMessage' failed! (400: Bad Request: message thread not found)
  invalidTopicSend=failed_closed topic=999999
  invalidTopicCheck=no_threadless_retry_observed

  nonGeneralTopicProof=head=f515e5d36ac1166b955838c21489390dbfd9dbe2
  nonGeneralTopic=16
  [telegram] outbound send ok accountId=default chatId=-1003908474243 messageId=17 operation=sendMessage deliveryKind=text threadId=16 chunkCount=1
  nonGeneralTopicSend=ok messageId=17 chatId=-1003908474243 topic=16
  ```
- Observed result after fix: Root chat-scoped send succeeded without a topic/thread id; General topic delivery succeeded and remained chat-scoped; an invalid explicit non-General forum topic failed closed and did not retry threadless; a valid explicit non-General forum topic sent with `threadId=16`, proving delivery stayed in the requested forum topic.
- What was not tested: I did not run a full live cron or heartbeat follow-up through the gateway in this proof pass. Existing focused tests still cover cron session delivery context and heartbeat topic-route reconstruction.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The live proof covers the real Telegram transport and topic send path. Cron/heartbeat route reconstruction remains covered by focused regression tests rather than a scheduled end-to-end cron/heartbeat run.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ClawSweeper's current-main/source review for this PR identifies the pre-patch failure mode: explicit Telegram topic sends could retry threadless after `message thread not found`, and async follow-up routing could lose topic context. The after-fix proof above shows the explicit send path no longer retries threadless for an invalid non-General topic.

## Verification

Current head `75acdc52684b07c8355966394f5ad5347a713e0a` was refreshed against latest `origin/main`, trimmed back to the intended nine-file effective diff, and pushed after the commands above passed locally.

## Security Impact

Reduces cross-topic message leakage in Telegram forum groups and private/direct topic contexts by failing closed when an explicit thread is stale or invalid, and by preserving topic context for automatic follow-up notices.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config migration. Non-General forum topic and direct/private topic sends now surface the Telegram error instead of silently retrying threadless. Explicit cron announce routes now persist the intended delivery context for later automatic notices.

## Risks

If an operator intentionally relied on stale explicit topic sends falling back to a root chat, those sends now fail. That behavior was the leakage bug this PR is meant to prevent.

The cron/session change is intentionally limited to resolved explicit delivery targets; bare `delivery.mode: none` jobs still avoid implicit last-target resolution.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted refresh, simplification, validation, and PR-body update.

target.title: feat(plugins): add system-tier plugin discovery for machine-wide policy  enforcement"
target.body:
## Summary

  - Adds a new `"system"` plugin origin loaded from a machine-wide directory (`/etc/openclaw/plugins` on Linux/Mac,
  `%ProgramData%\OpenClaw\plugins` on Windows)
  - System plugins are immune to user config (deny/allow lists, per-entry disable, global toggle)
  - System plugins get implicit `allowConversationAccess` and `allowPromptInjection` permissions
  - System plugin hooks run at priority >= 10000 (before all user/bundled hooks)

  This enables SYSTEM services to deploy policy-enforcement plugins that intercept the full agent loop (tool calls,
  prompts, model selection, outbound messages) without requiring user registration or CLI interaction.

  ## Changed files

  | File | Change |
  |---|---|
  | `plugin-origin.types.ts` | Added `"system"` to `PluginOrigin` union |
  | `roots.ts` | `resolveSystemPluginsDir()` with platform-aware defaults + test override setter |
  | `discovery.ts` | System tier scanned before all other sources |
  | `config-activation-shared.ts` | System origin bypasses all activation checks |
  | `registry.ts` | Implicit permissions + priority floor for system hooks |
  | `loader.ts` | System dir in cache key |
  | `source-display.ts` | Format system plugin sources in CLI output |

  ## Test plan

  - [x] `roots.test.ts` — 5 tests: platform resolution (Windows, Linux, macOS)
  - [x] `system-plugin-activation.test.ts` — 8 tests: bypass deny/allow/disable, non-system still blocked
  - [x] `system-plugin-discovery.test.ts` — 5 tests: discovery with origin=system, precedence, empty/missing dir
  - [x] `system-plugin-loader.test.ts` — 7 tests: full loader integration, permissions, priority ordering
  - [x] `system-plugin-blocking.test.ts` — 9 tests: tool call blocking, message cancellation, agent reply short-circuit,
   transcript suppression, terminal semantics
  - [x] Existing `loader.test.ts` — 122/122 pass (no regressions)
  - [x] Existing `discovery.test.ts` — 48 pass (no regressions)
  - [x] Existing `source-display.test.ts` — 4/4 pass (no regressions)
  - [x] Plugin contract tests — 734/734 pass
  - [x] Import boundary check — clean

  - [x] AI-assisted: built with Claude Code
  - [x] Degree of testing: **fully tested** (38 new tests + all existing pass)
  - [x] Confirm understanding of code changes

  🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(discord): preserve target intent during normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord-like explicit DM targets could be normalized through the id-like target path without preserving the intended direct-message kind.
- Why it matters: A Discord target that is syntactically a user/direct target should stay routed as a user/direct target instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What changed: Shared target-kind inference now lives in `target-normalization`, and both outbound target resolution and cron delivery pass the inferred kind into id-like plugin resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No Discord runtime sending behavior, config schema, plugin manifest, or user-facing docs changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Explicit Discord direct-message targets such as `user:<id>` preserve inferred user/direct intent when id-like target resolution runs for outbound and cron delivery.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows/PowerShell, Discord plugin configured with a real bot token and a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran local OpenClaw Discord outbound/cron delivery paths against an explicit `user:<id>` Discord target using the patched branch, then compared the live delivery result with the new resolver/cron coverage.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Existing live-result report from @AScriver after the local Discord run, with private IDs and tokens redacted:

```text
Scenario: Discord outbound/cron delivery to an explicit user:<id> target
Result: delivered to the intended DM recipient and preserved explicit user target intent instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference
Coverage alignment: matches the targeted resolver and cron tests added in this PR
```

- Observed result after fix: Discord delivered to the intended DM recipient, and the explicit user/direct target intent was preserved instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What was not tested: Raw Discord token and private IDs are not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Source-level before-state showed the id-like resolver did not receive inferred `preferredKind` for outbound/cron paths; the targeted regression tests cover that missing handoff.
## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: id-like target resolution received only the raw target string, so the plugin resolver could lose the caller's inferred direct-message intent after normalization.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered id-like fallback and directory inference separately, but not propagation of inferred DM/user kind into plugin id-like resolution.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord target parsing can distinguish user/direct targets before the generic id-like normalization path runs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: inferred direct-message/user kind is passed into id-like plugin target resolution.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver seam and cron delivery call path without needing a live Discord bot.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord-targeted delivery paths preserve explicit direct-message intent more reliably when resolving id-like targets. No config or command syntax changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord DM-like target -> generic id-like resolution -> kind may default away from user/direct intent

After:
Discord DM-like target -> infer user/direct kind -> id-like resolution receives preferredKind=user -> direct target preserved
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows, PowerShell
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout, Node/pnpm from workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord target routing logic
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Resolve a plugin id-like target whose channel messaging adapter infers `direct` chat type.
2. Resolve a cron delivery target for an explicit DM-like target.
3. Assert the id-like resolver receives `preferredKind: "user"` and preserves the returned target.

### Expected

- The id-like plugin resolver receives the inferred user/direct kind.
- Cron delivery preserves explicit DM intent after target normalization.

### Actual

- Verified by the new focused tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted validation:

```bash
pnpm test src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts
pnpm check:changed
```

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local Discord outbound/cron delivery to a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target was reported after this patch; target resolver and cron delivery target unit/seam coverage also passed locally.
- Edge cases checked: id-like raw targets continue passing `preferredKind: "group"`; explicit DM-like targets pass `preferredKind: "user"`.
- What I did **not** verify: raw Discord token/private IDs are intentionally not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: shared target kind inference could affect non-Discord channels that expose `inferTargetChatType`.
  - Mitigation: the helper preserves existing fallback behavior, maps `channel` to `group` where directory lookup expects groups, and adds focused coverage for the new id-like propagation path.

target.title: fix(discord): preserve content-type and skip optimization for animated/WebP images
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Discord media uploads were sending files without a MIME content-type, causing some clients/bots to misidentify the file format. Additionally, WebP images were being converted to JPEG (losing quality) and animated images (GIF, WebP, APNG) were being converted/optimized, destroying animation frames.
- **Why it matters:** Users sending WebP images or animated GIFs/APNGs via Discord and the web provider received degraded or broken output — silent data-loss bugs.
- **What changed:** `toDiscordFileBlob` now accepts and forwards a `contentType` to the `Blob` constructor; `loadWebMediaInternal` skips image optimization for GIF, WebP, and any animated image (detected via sharp's `pages` metadata); a new `isAnimatedImage` helper was added to `image-ops.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** Non-animated JPEG/PNG optimization paths are unchanged; no other channels are affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #41256
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- WebP images sent via Discord or the web provider are no longer silently converted to JPEG.
- Animated GIFs and animated WebP/APNG files are no longer passed through the image optimizer (which would strip frames).
- Discord uploads now include the correct MIME type in the `Blob`, so recipients see the right file format.
- Error messages for oversized GIF/WebP/animated images now include a more specific label (`GIF`, `WebP`, `Animated image`) instead of the generic `Media`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No

target.title: fix(agents): detect irreducible context overflow to prevent compaction loop DoS
target.body:
Add circuit breaker for irreducible context overflow and exhausted compaction attempts.

When an agent's system prompt + user prompt alone exceeds the model's context window, compaction
cannot reduce the overflow because it only removes session history messages — the system prompt is
fixed-size and irreducible. Previously, the precheck would route to `compact_only`, causing the
run loop to burn synchronous event loop time per compaction attempt (up to 3), then
trigger a session reset. On the next incoming message, the cycle repeats with a fresh
counter, effectively creating an infinite DoS loop that starves the entire gateway event loop.

The fix adds two new terminal error kinds:
- `irreducible_overflow`: when the baseline token estimate already exceeds the context budget.
- `context_overflow_exhausted`: when compaction attempts are exceeded without resolving the overflow.

The `runAgentTurnWithFallback` logic has been updated to check for these specific errors. If they occur, it now prevents triggering a session reset, surfacing the error directly to the user rather than entering a perpetual restart loop.

**Files changed:**
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/types.ts` — added `context_overflow_exhausted` and `irreducible_overflow` error kinds
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.types.ts` — new `irreducible_overflow` route type
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts` — immediate error return for irreducible overflows and exhausted attempts
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` — explicitly check for terminal errors and bypass session reset
- `CHANGELOG.md` — documented the prevention of the irreducible overflow loop
- Tests: `run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts`, `run.overflow-compaction.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:**
Prevents infinite compaction loops when system prompts exceed the model's max context window or when compaction fails to reduce context sufficiently.

**Environment tested:**
Tested via unit tests and E2E simulation.

**Evidence:**
Unit tests verify that `runEmbeddedPiAgent` surfaces `irreducible_overflow` or `context_overflow_exhausted`, and `runAgentTurnWithFallback` catches these and skips session reset.

**Observed result:**
Instead of freezing the gateway with 100% CPU loops or perpetual session resets, the agent immediately rejects the request with an actionable error.

target.title: fix: isolate WhatsApp direct sessions by account
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the WhatsApp direct-message context leak reported in #76263. With the default shared DM scope, inbound WhatsApp DMs could collapse into the agent main session, so distinct contacts shared one session file and model context.

## Root Cause

WhatsApp inbound routing passed a direct peer to the generic route resolver, but generic routing defaults `session.dmScope` to `main`. That default is useful for personal single-user continuity, but unsafe for WhatsApp shared inbox and SaaS deployments where many senders contact the same account.

## What Changed

- Adds a per-call `dmScopeOverride` to `resolveAgentRoute()` so channel plugins can request stricter DM isolation without changing the global config contract.
- Makes WhatsApp inbound direct chats use account-aware per-contact session keys: `agent:<agentId>:whatsapp:<accountId>:direct:<peerId>`.
- Preserves WhatsApp group session behavior.
- Records last-route metadata on the isolated DM session key, so follow-up delivery context stays aligned with the new session.
- Updates WhatsApp docs and changelog to document the new direct-chat session contract.

## Why Account-Aware

The earlier bundled PR used `per-channel-peer`, which isolated different contacts on one WhatsApp account but collapsed the same sender across multiple WhatsApp accounts. This PR uses `per-account-channel-peer` for WhatsApp DMs so both dimensions are isolated.

## Scope

WhatsApp inbound DM session routing and last-route metadata only. No outbound newsletter behavior, group session keys, allowlists, auth, provider/model behavior, or non-WhatsApp channel defaults are changed.

## Test Plan

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/routing/resolve-route.ts src/routing/resolve-route.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/on-message.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.last-route.test.ts docs/channels/whatsapp.md CHANGELOG.md`
- `pnpm test src/routing/resolve-route.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.last-route.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- GitHub PR CI: green on `6523eb7618b463c249b3b557aa124ffbe6379a06`

## Linked Issue / Replacement

Fixes #76263.

Replaces the WhatsApp-only portion of closed bundled PR #76326.

target.title: feat(telegram): add acknowledgement sticker support
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram does not support streaming text, so users get no immediate interactive feedback while OpenClaw generates a response.
- Why it matters: Ack reactions help, but stickers are more visible and expressive for Telegram conversations.
- What changed: Added configurable Telegram ack stickers with scope control, optional silent delivery, optional removal after reply, config schema/docs, SDK feedback support, and Telegram tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Existing ack reaction behavior remains supported; this does not add text streaming to Telegram or change default Telegram access policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram lacks streaming text support, so acknowledgement feedback is handled through channel feedback primitives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/identity.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Telegram resolves ackSticker config, sends the configured sticker before dispatching the agent turn, and optionally removes it after reply.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers config resolution, channel feedback shape, Telegram send behavior, and dispatch cleanup without requiring live Telegram credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing ack reaction tests covered reaction behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram users can configure `ackSticker` globally, per account, per direct/group chat, or per topic. When enabled, OpenClaw sends a Telegram sticker acknowledgement before generating the response. Existing ack reactions continue to work.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Telegram message -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply

After:
Telegram message -> ack sticker -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply -> optional ack sticker cleanup
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Telegram may call `sendSticker` and optionally delete that sticker message. This uses the existing configured Telegram bot token and existing channel access controls; no new credential source or sender authorization path is added.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: Not required for unit coverage
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      enabled: true,
      ackSticker: {
        fileId: "CAAC...",
        scope: "direct",
        silent: true,
        removeAfterReply: true,
      },
    },
  },
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure Telegram `ackSticker.fileId` with a sendable Telegram sticker file id.
2. Start the OpenClaw gateway with Telegram enabled.
3. Send a DM to the Telegram bot.

### Expected

- Telegram sends the configured acknowledgement sticker before the agent response.
- The agent response is delivered normally.
- If `removeAfterReply` is enabled, the acknowledgement sticker is deleted after the final reply.

### Actual

- Unit and config tests pass for the implemented behavior.
- Live Telegram verification was attempted locally but blocked by local gateway token/env setup issues, not by the ackSticker implementation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation run:

```sh
pnpm test src/agents/identity.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm config:channels:check
pnpm config:docs:gen
pnpm config:docs:check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed-ts-files>
git diff --check
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Config schema accepts ackSticker fields.
  - Channel feedback carries ackSticker configuration.
  - Telegram send path sends stickers and respects notification options.
  - Telegram dispatch path can clean up ack stickers after reply.
  - Docs/generated config baseline are updated.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Scope resolution mirrors ack reaction behavior.
  - Sticker cleanup is best-effort.
  - Ack reaction remains available and can be disabled independently.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live Telegram round-trip with a real bot after local launchd token setup issues.
  - Broad full-suite CI locally.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Existing configs continue to work. Users can opt in by adding `channels.telegram.ackSticker` or `messages.ackSticker`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Invalid Telegram sticker `file_id` causes the acknowledgement sticker send to fail.
  - Mitigation: Ack sticker send failure is isolated from final reply delivery.
- Risk: Sticker deletion can fail due to Telegram permissions or timing.
  - Mitigation: Cleanup is best-effort and does not block final response delivery.

target.title: fix(matrix): add async shared-client stop helpers awaiting final persist
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The synchronous `stopSharedClient`, `stopSharedClientForAccount`, and `stopSharedClientInstance` helpers in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts` kick off the final crypto/sync persist via `MatrixClient.stop()` but return before it completes, so a process exit right after a stop can lose recently negotiated Olm/Megolm session keys.
- Why it matters: Lost crypto state shows up after restart as `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops and repeated "Established new Olm sessions" against the same sender.
- What changed: Added async `stopSharedClientAsync`, `stopSharedClientForAccountAsync`, and `stopSharedClientInstanceAsync` that await `client.stopAndPersist()`. Marked the legacy synchronous helpers `@deprecated`. Re-exported the two account-scoped async helpers from the matrix barrel. Added three Vitest cases. Added one CHANGELOG line.
- What did NOT change: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts` was not touched. `MatrixClient.stop()` already triggers the final persist and `stopAndPersist()` already exists; the periodic 60s timer and the four crypto-event bindings at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/decrypt-bridge.ts:153-164` were already wired.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #76611 
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: this PR narrowly fixes the synchronous stop-helper persistence defect from #76611 only. The event-driven crypto-state persist defect (`KeyBackupDecryptionKeyCached`, `RehydrationCompleted`, `DevicesUpdated`, `KeysChanged` not driving `persistIdbToDisk`) remains open and is not addressed here.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Sync helpers return `void` and call `client.stop()`, which assigns `stopPersistPromise` for the final IDB/sync flush but does not return it.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test pinned that shared-client teardown produces a settled persist promise; no `@deprecated` signal steered callers toward the existing async path.
- Contributing context: `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` and `MatrixClient.stopAndPersist()` already exist as the safe path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`
- Scenario: Each new async helper invokes `client.stopAndPersist()` exactly once on the targeted client(s) and does not call `client.stop()`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The shared-client registry is the single chokepoint between callers and `MatrixClient`.
- Existing test that already covers this: None for the async-stop helpers.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None for end users. Plugin authors who deep-import `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client.ts` get three new async exports; legacy synchronous exports keep their current signatures.

## Diagram

N/A.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Net effect on security posture is positive: hardens the Matrix E2EE crypto-store shutdown path so recently negotiated session keys are durable across restarts when the new helpers are adopted.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11; fix is platform-agnostic.
- Runtime: Node 22.
- Integration: Matrix (`extensions/matrix`).

### Steps

1. Acquire a shared Matrix client through `resolveSharedMatrixClient` for an E2EE-enabled account.
2. Trigger a shutdown path that calls a synchronous `stopSharedClient*` helper and exits the process immediately.
3. Restart and observe whether incoming E2EE messages decrypt cleanly.

### Expected

- Recently negotiated Olm/Megolm sessions remain decryptable across restart.

### Actual (without this fix)

- Process exits before `stopPersistPromise` resolves; restart loses recent session keys, producing `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

Three Vitest cases added in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`. CI must run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed`.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Static review against the existing `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` precedent at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts:295`. Verified the new helpers reuse `deleteSharedClientState` and `removeSharedClientInstance` with the same ordering as the existing async-release path. Verified the mock client in `shared.test.ts` exposes `stopAndPersist` without breaking the existing release-lease test.
- Edge cases checked: Concurrent acquire after a stop drops cleanly because state is removed before the awaited persist. `Promise.all` was chosen for `stopSharedClientAsync` to match the existing error semantics; inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)` at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts:677-689`.
- What was not verified: Vitest was not executed in the authoring shell (no `pnpm`/`node` on PATH). The reporter's full prod symptom was not reproduced against a live homeserver.

## Review Conversations

- [x] Bot review conversations addressed in this PR will be replied to or resolved.
- [x] Only conversations that still need maintainer judgment will be left open.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A future contributor copies a `@deprecated` synchronous helper into a new shutdown path despite the jsdoc warning.
  - Mitigation: jsdoc on each deprecated helper names the async replacement directly; CHANGELOG entry highlights the shutdown-correctness rationale.
- Risk: `Promise.all` in `stopSharedClientAsync` rejects on the first failing per-client persist.
  - Mitigation: Inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)`, so `stopAndPersist()` resolves under normal conditions; behavior matches `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")`.

---

AI-assisted PR (Claude Opus 4.7, 1M context). Lightly tested: static review and trace-through; Vitest not executed locally. Reviewers should run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed` before landing.

target.body:
## Summary

Adds a `<Warning>` callout to the Prometheus Quick Start clarifying that `plugins.allow` is **strict-mode** — when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load. The current Quick Start example shows `allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` without warning that this disables every other configured plugin on a real running gateway.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR was drafted with **Claude Code** (Anthropic's CLI agent).

- [x] **AI-assisted** — drafted by Claude Code (this section makes it explicit; trailing footer also marks the agent)
- [x] **Testing**: lightly tested. Empirically reproduced the failure mode that motivates this Warning — `plugins.allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` dropped a 72-plugin running v2026.5.2 gateway to 9 enabled plugins; recovered via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + gateway restart. Did NOT build the docs site locally — render check left for CI / maintainer.
- [x] **Author understanding** — confirmed source-level grasp of the activation flow (`src/plugins/config-activation-shared.ts` `resolvePluginActivationDecisionShared`), the three narrow exceptions to `plugins.allow` (`selected-memory-slot`, `selected-context-engine-slot`, conditional `bundled-channel-enabled-in-config`), and the capability gate at `services-CTQW_M_S.js:15` that motivated cross-referenced #76628.
- [ ] **`codex review --base origin/main` not run locally** — Codex CLI is not installed in this contributor environment. Relying on the GitHub Codex bot (clawsweeper) auto-review run that posted to this PR as the AI review pass.
- [x] **Bot review conversations** — clawsweeper Codex review on this PR addressed in a follow-up comment below (covers the soft suggestion about example tuning).

## Why

Following the Quick Start example as-written on a multi-plugin gateway (e.g. one running `memory-core`, `active-memory`, `memory-wiki`, `telegram`, etc.) silently flips the gateway into strict-allowlist mode and drops all other configured plugins. On a 71-plugin running gateway (mid-sized self-hosted setup), the example dropped the enabled-plugin count from 72 to **9** — including critical plugins like `memory-core`, `active-memory`, and the configured Telegram channel — until rolled back via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow`.

The warning surface for this is misleading in two ways:

1. The startup log line `[plugins] plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: <id>. Set plugins.allow to explicit trusted ids.` *implies* setting `plugins.allow` is the right next step. It isn't, in the general case.
2. The schema description for `plugins.allow` is correct (*"when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load"*), but the Quick Start snippet doesn't reproduce that semantics in user-visible documentation.

This PR adds a single `<Warning>` callout next to the snippet, naming the failure mode explicitly so users on existing multi-plugin gateways don't trip the regression.

## Cross-references

- **#75575** (open) — *Honor `plugins.allow` on runtime provider paths (currently force-loaded) — propose `plugins.bundledMode` opt-in*. Same family of `plugins.allow` semantic confusion. The Warning links to that issue for users who want the longer architectural discussion.
- **#76628** (just filed) — *@openclaw/diagnostics-prometheus loads via npm but internalDiagnostics capability is bundled-only* — describes a separate but adjacent failure mode in the same Quick Start path; mentions this PR's `plugins.allow` finding as one of the empirical investigations along the way.

## Scope

- Single-file doc change: `docs/gateway/prometheus.md`
- No code changes
- No behavior changes
- Backward compatible (callouts are additive)

## Test plan

- [x] Source-level check: `<Warning>` is the same MDX component already used elsewhere in the same file (`docs/gateway/prometheus.md:19-21`), so no new component dependencies. Indentation matches the surrounding `<Step>` / `<Tabs>` block.
- [ ] Docs preview render — left for maintainer/CI; I have not built the docs site locally.
- [x] Empirically reproduced the regression that this Warning describes (multi-plugin gateway dropping from 72 → 9 enabled plugins after applying the existing snippet, fully recovered by `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + restart). See **#76628** for the full reproduction.

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: fix(comfy): match Comfy Cloud API completion status values
target.body:
## Summary

Comfy Cloud API returns `"success"` for completed workflows, but OpenClaw only checked for `"completed"`. This caused workflows that succeeded on Comfy Cloud to time out after 300s in OpenClaw.

Additionally, Comfy Cloud uses `"error"` as a terminal failure status, which was not handled.

## Changes

- Accept `"success"` as a completion status (in addition to `"completed"`)
- Treat `"error"` as a terminal failure status (in addition to `"failed"` and `"cancelled"`)

## Test plan

- [ ] Comfy Cloud image generation completes without 300s timeout
- [ ] Failed/error workflows still throw appropriate errors

target.title: fix(zalouser): split messages at line boundaries instead of mid-word
target.body:
## TL;DR

Long bot responses were split mid-word because the default chunk mode cut at exactly 2000 characters with no regard for word or sentence boundaries.

---

## Problem

`DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_MODE = "length"` cuts the outgoing message at exactly the 2000-character limit regardless of whether that lands in the middle of a word. In practice this was observed as a Vietnamese word like "lên" arriving as two separate messages — "lê" followed by "n" — which is unreadable.

The `"newline"` chunk mode already exists in the codebase and correctly breaks at paragraph boundaries → single newlines → whitespace → hard cut (as a last resort). It was never set as the default, so callers that did not explicitly pass `textChunkMode: "newline"` got the hard-cut behavior.

---

## Invariant

> When a message is split into chunks, each chunk boundary must fall at a natural break (paragraph, newline, or whitespace) unless no such break exists within the limit window.

---

## What this PR does

- Changes `DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_MODE` from `"length"` to `"newline"` in `send.ts`
- Adds one regression test: a message with a paragraph break inside the 2000-char window is split at the break, not at position 2000

---

## Why this matters

- **User impact**: Words arrived split across two Zalo messages, making bot responses unreadable in long replies.
- **System impact**: The hard-cut behavior never had a use case — `"newline"` falls back to a hard cut when no natural break exists, so it is strictly better for all inputs.

---

## Safety

- The `splitTextRangesByPreferredBreaks` function (used by `"newline"` mode) falls back to a hard cut when no natural break point is found, so behavior for texts with no whitespace is unchanged.
- All existing tests pass unchanged: they use continuous character strings with no natural break points, which fall through to the same hard-cut behavior as before.
- No change to any other logic.

---

## Test plan

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/zalouser/src/send.test.ts
```

- [x] All existing tests pass (13)
- [x] New test: paragraph break within window → split at break, not at position 2000

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
Fixes #90011
Fixes #82304

## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers clean up temp files, show a warning, and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Preserve signal exit codes through `run_quiet_step`**: the wrapper previously normalized all failures to exit 1, which swallowed SIGINT (130). Now returns signal exit codes (>128) so the doctor exit-130 check fires correctly, while preserving exit 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics across all 66+ installer call sites
4. **Clear dashboard flag on doctor failure**: when the interactive upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1, SIGINT exit 130, or ordinary failure), clear `should_open_dashboard` so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard after an incomplete upgrade
5. **Guard every run_doctor caller**: the existing-config fresh-install path called `run_doctor` without checking its return value, so a failed doctor would still set `should_open_dashboard=true`. Now guarded with `if run_doctor; then should_open_dashboard=true; fi`
6. **Fix Clack cancel exit code**: `guardCancel` in `onboard-helpers.ts` called `runtime.exit(0)` when the user cancelled at a Clack prompt (Escape key), making the install script treat cancellation as success. Changed to `runtime.exit(1)` to match the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` and `onboard-interactive.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** (1) Pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer; the installer continues and opens a dead dashboard. (2) `guardCancel` in onboard-helpers exited with 0/1 instead of 130, so the installer could not distinguish user cancellation from regular failure.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node v26.3.0, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/wt-76386`. Gateway started and doctor command exercised on a real OpenClaw deployment.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Built from branch: `CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm build`

  Step 2. Verified the fix in the compiled production bundle (before vs after).

  Step 3. Started the patched gateway and ran `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` to confirm the doctor module works correctly.

  Step 4. Sent SIGINT to the installer during a real upgrade to verify the abort path.

  Step 5. Ran the full install script test suite (49 tests).

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

  **Before (upstream/main):** `guardCancel` exits with 0, making the installer treat Clack cancellation as success:

  ```console
  $ sed -n '27,31p' /tmp/oc-base/dist/onboard-helpers-DxyrQbNM.js
  function guardCancel(value, runtime) {
  if (isCancel(value)) {
  cancel(stylePromptTitle("Setup cancelled.") ?? "Setup cancelled.");
  runtime.exit(0);
  throw new Error("unreachable");
  ```

  **After (patched):** `guardCancel` exits with 130 (SIGINT convention), which the installer detects and aborts:

  ```console
  $ sed -n '27,31p' /tmp/wt-76386/dist/onboard-helpers-D47vq5rt.js
  function guardCancel(value, runtime) {
  if (isCancel(value)) {
  cancel(stylePromptTitle("Setup cancelled.") ?? "Setup cancelled.");
  runtime.exit(130);
  throw new Error("unreachable");
  ```

  **Install script `run_doctor` now detects exit 130:**

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'doctor_exit.*130\|abort_install_int' /tmp/wt-76386/scripts/install.sh
  35:abort_install_int() {
  47:trap abort_install_int INT
  2835:    if (( doctor_exit == 130 )); then
  2836:        abort_install_int
  3303:            if (( doctor_exit == 130 )); then
  ```

  **Doctor runs correctly on the live deployment:**

  ```console
  $ cd /tmp/wt-76386 && timeout 5 node openclaw.mjs doctor --non-interactive
  OPENCLAW
  OpenClaw doctor
  Doctor warnings
    - Left migrated plugin install index in place because archive already exists
  [state-migrations] Legacy state migration warnings:
    - Left migrated plugin install index in place
  $ echo "exit code: $?"
  exit code: 0
  ```

  **Gateway starts cleanly with the patched module:**

  ```console
  $ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
  [gateway] http server listening (9 plugins; 1.0s)
  [gateway] ready
  [shutdown] completed cleanly in 77ms
  ```

  **SIGINT during install triggers clean abort:**

  ```console
  $ set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"
  Preparing installer interface...
    OpenClaw Installer
  Detected: macos
  [1/3] Preparing environment
  Node.js found
  [2/3] Installing OpenClaw
  ! Installation interrupted
  exit=130
  ```

  **All 49 install script tests pass:**

  ```console
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts
   ✓ |tooling| install-sh.test.ts (49 tests) 4056ms
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  49 passed (49)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The compiled production code at `dist/onboard-helpers-D47vq5rt.js:30` calls `runtime.exit(130)` instead of `runtime.exit(0)` when the user cancels at a Clack prompt. The install script at line 2835 detects `doctor_exit == 130` and calls `abort_install_int`, which exits with 130 and the "Installation interrupted" message. Terminal output confirms: (1) doctor runs correctly on the live deployment, (2) SIGINT during install triggers clean abort with exit 130, (3) the gateway loads the patched module without errors.
- **What was not tested:** Live interactive Ctrl+C at a real Clack upgrade-doctor prompt (requires a state where doctor triggers an interactive migration question). The exit code propagation from `guardCancel(130)` through `run_doctor` to `abort_install_int` was verified via the compiled bundle, install script grep, and the 49-test suite.

target.title: fix(exec): hide policy controls from model args
target.body:
## Summary

- Remove operator-only exec controls (`security`, `ask`, `host`, `elevated`, `node`) from the model-facing exec tool schema
- Stop consuming those fields from exec tool-call args; configured tool defaults and approval defaults now remain the only policy source
- Move trusted chat command policy overrides into exec defaults instead of per-call args
- Update regression coverage for model-supplied policy fields, configured host/node routing, elevated defaults, and agent-specific exec defaults

Fixes #75811.

## Security

This closes the model-controlled exec policy boundary described in #75811. Model tool calls can still provide command/runtime inputs (`command`, `workdir`, `env`, `yieldMs`, `background`, `timeout`, `pty`), but cannot lower/raise security, approval, host routing, elevation, or node selection through tool arguments.

## Test plan

- pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.schemas.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.exec.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.schema.test.ts
- pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts
- pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.schemas.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.exec.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/bash-command.stop.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/bash-tools.schemas.ts src/agents/bash-tools.schemas.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.exec.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/bash-command.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-diagnostics.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-export-trajectory.ts

target.title: [codex] Fallback when Windows gateway task exits early
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a Windows scheduled-task fallback for the failure shape from Brad's workshop log: `schtasks /Run` is accepted, but the task quickly reports `Last Run Result=0` / stopped and no gateway listener appears.

Previously the fallback only handled the "accepted but never starts" state (`0x41303`). This extends it to the early clean-exit/no-listener case by briefly polling for launch evidence, then launching the gateway script directly via the existing Startup-style detached fallback.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/daemon/schtasks.startup-fallback.test.ts src/daemon/schtasks.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/daemon/schtasks.ts src/daemon/schtasks.startup-fallback.test.ts`
