commands.author: AScriver

target.title: fix(discord): preserve target intent during normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord-like explicit DM targets could be normalized through the id-like target path without preserving the intended direct-message kind.
- Why it matters: A Discord target that is syntactically a user/direct target should stay routed as a user/direct target instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What changed: Shared target-kind inference now lives in `target-normalization`, and both outbound target resolution and cron delivery pass the inferred kind into id-like plugin resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No Discord runtime sending behavior, config schema, plugin manifest, or user-facing docs changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Explicit Discord direct-message targets such as `user:<id>` preserve inferred user/direct intent when id-like target resolution runs for outbound and cron delivery.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows/PowerShell, Discord plugin configured with a real bot token and a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran local OpenClaw Discord outbound/cron delivery paths against an explicit `user:<id>` Discord target using the patched branch, then compared the live delivery result with the new resolver/cron coverage.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Existing live-result report from @AScriver after the local Discord run, with private IDs and tokens redacted:

```text
Scenario: Discord outbound/cron delivery to an explicit user:<id> target
Result: delivered to the intended DM recipient and preserved explicit user target intent instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference
Coverage alignment: matches the targeted resolver and cron tests added in this PR
```

- Observed result after fix: Discord delivered to the intended DM recipient, and the explicit user/direct target intent was preserved instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What was not tested: Raw Discord token and private IDs are not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Source-level before-state showed the id-like resolver did not receive inferred `preferredKind` for outbound/cron paths; the targeted regression tests cover that missing handoff.
## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: id-like target resolution received only the raw target string, so the plugin resolver could lose the caller's inferred direct-message intent after normalization.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered id-like fallback and directory inference separately, but not propagation of inferred DM/user kind into plugin id-like resolution.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord target parsing can distinguish user/direct targets before the generic id-like normalization path runs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: inferred direct-message/user kind is passed into id-like plugin target resolution.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver seam and cron delivery call path without needing a live Discord bot.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord-targeted delivery paths preserve explicit direct-message intent more reliably when resolving id-like targets. No config or command syntax changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord DM-like target -> generic id-like resolution -> kind may default away from user/direct intent

After:
Discord DM-like target -> infer user/direct kind -> id-like resolution receives preferredKind=user -> direct target preserved
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows, PowerShell
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout, Node/pnpm from workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord target routing logic
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Resolve a plugin id-like target whose channel messaging adapter infers `direct` chat type.
2. Resolve a cron delivery target for an explicit DM-like target.
3. Assert the id-like resolver receives `preferredKind: "user"` and preserves the returned target.

### Expected

- The id-like plugin resolver receives the inferred user/direct kind.
- Cron delivery preserves explicit DM intent after target normalization.

### Actual

- Verified by the new focused tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted validation:

```bash
pnpm test src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts
pnpm check:changed
```

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local Discord outbound/cron delivery to a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target was reported after this patch; target resolver and cron delivery target unit/seam coverage also passed locally.
- Edge cases checked: id-like raw targets continue passing `preferredKind: "group"`; explicit DM-like targets pass `preferredKind: "user"`.
- What I did **not** verify: raw Discord token/private IDs are intentionally not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.

commands.author: Nas01010101

target.title: fix(sessions): preserve Media* index alignment when reading user-turn fields
target.body:
## Summary

The inbound-media **writer** pads missing values with `""` to keep the parallel `MediaPaths` / `MediaUrls` / `MediaTypes` arrays index-aligned, but the user-turn-transcript **reader** compacted those `""` holes with `.filter(Boolean)` before indexing. The two arrays then drift out of alignment, so a later attachment's content-type (or URL) is assigned to an **earlier** attachment in the persisted transcript.

Fixes #94255

## Change

`normalizeOptionalTextArray` in `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.ts` no longer compacts. It now maps each entry to a normalized string or `undefined` and returns `(string | undefined)[]`, preserving the writer's `""` alignment holes (which normalize to `undefined`) so index `i` keeps referring to the same attachment.

The downstream builder (`buildPersistedUserTurnMediaInputsFromFields`) already does per-index `??` lookups (`types[index] ?? (index === 0 ? singleType : undefined)`) and derives `mediaCount` from `Math.max(paths.length, urls.length, ...)`, so it treats an `undefined` hole as "absent" with no further change — and `mediaCount` is now the true attachment count instead of a post-compaction shrink.

**Reviewer decision points:**
- Return type widened from `string[]` to `(string | undefined)[]`. The only call site is the builder above; the `paths[index]`/`urls[index]`/`types[index]` lookups already tolerate `undefined` via `??`, and the `if (!mediaPath && !url) continue;` guard skips pure-hole rows.
- No change to the single-field fallbacks (`MediaPath`/`MediaUrl`/`MediaType` at index 0).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** An inbound media turn with two attachments where the first lacks a content-type (`MediaTypes = ["", "image/png"]`) — the persisted reader assigned `image/png` to the typeless first attachment instead of to the second.

**Real environment tested:** openclaw worktree at HEAD of `main` (`a7f96847ce`) on macOS, Node v26.0.0, exercising the real exported `buildPersistedUserTurnMediaInputsFromFields` reader (no mocks) against the actual writer wire format `MediaTypes=["","image/png"]`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** added a temporary spec calling the real reader and printed its output, run via the repo's vitest with `node` so console output surfaces:

```
node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/sessions/_proof.test.ts --config vitest.config.ts --disable-console-intercept
```

**Evidence after fix:** real reader console output, copied verbatim:

```
INPUT MediaPaths= ["/media/a.bin","/media/b.png"]
INPUT MediaTypes= ["","image/png"]
OUT /media/a.bin => undefined
OUT /media/b.png => image/png
```

For contrast, the same real reader on the pre-patch source produced the misaligned output:

```
OUT /media/a.bin => image/png
OUT /media/b.png => image/png
```

**Observed result after fix:** `/media/a.bin` now resolves to `undefined` (no false content-type borrowed from the next attachment), and `/media/b.png` keeps its own `image/png`. Before the patch `/media/a.bin` was wrongly labeled `image/png`. Each attachment now resolves independently from its own index.

**What was not tested:** end-to-end channel ingestion through a live messaging provider (the fix is confined to the persisted-fields reader). The `MediaUrls` misalignment variant is covered by the unit suite rather than a live capture.

## Tests and validation

Two regression tests added to `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts` (the `MediaTypes` hole case and the `MediaUrls` hole case). Full file suite: **27/27 pass**. oxlint and tsgo clean on the changed file. The temporary proof spec shown above was removed after capturing the evidence; it is not part of the diff.

target.title: fix(redact): stop over-masking kebab identifiers as Apple app-specific passwords
target.body:
## Summary

`redactSensitiveFieldValue()` / `redactSecrets()` (`src/logging/redact.ts`) over-mask ordinary `aaaa-bbbb-cccc-dddd` kebab-case identifiers as suspected Apple app-specific passwords. `STRUCTURED_APP_PASSWORD_FIELD_RE` included the generic field names `text|content|message|error|errorMessage|detail|details|reason`, so any run of four hyphen-separated 4-letter lowercase segments (e.g. `help-desk-team-page`, `kube-node-pool-spec`) appearing in those fields was silently masked — and the corruption is persisted into the session transcript via `redactSecrets()` at `src/config/sessions/transcript-append.ts:577`. This is a data-integrity bug (it masks *more* than it should, never less), not a secret leak.

Fixes #94254

## Change

Minimal, and mirrors the existing Discord-bot-token pattern's nearby-literal anchor:

1. Drop the generic field names from `STRUCTURED_APP_PASSWORD_FIELD_RE`, leaving only the apple/icloud-specific names (`apple|icloud|app-specific-password|appSpecificPassword|application-password`) that gate the sweep unconditionally.
2. Add `APP_PASSWORD_CONTEXT_RE` + `APP_PASSWORD_CONTEXT_WINDOW` (50 chars) so that in a generic field the sweep still fires when an apple/icloud keyword appears within the window of the candidate — exactly how the Discord-token pattern requires the literal `discord` within 40 chars.
3. `redactAppSpecificPasswords()` now takes the field key: apple/icloud field → unconditional mask; otherwise → require the context anchor.

The context window is read from the **chunk-local string** that `replacePatternBounded` (`src/logging/redact-bounded.ts`) passes to the replacer, not the closed-over whole value — so the proximity check stays correct even when large inputs (>32 KiB) are processed in 16 KiB chunks. No behavior change for real Apple app passwords: they are still masked in apple/icloud-named fields, and in generic text whenever the surrounding text mentions iCloud/Apple/app password.

**Reviewer decision points:** the context window is 50 chars (Discord uses 40), chosen so a leading `iCloud rejected ` / `app password ` prefix comfortably fits before the token. A token within 50 chars of a 16 KiB chunk boundary may miss a context keyword on the far side of that boundary — a deliberately-accepted false-negative (it under-masks a maybe-secret, never over-masks innocent text), and such tokens are still caught by the apple/icloud field-name gate that the structured app-password fields use.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** kebab-case identifiers (`help-desk-team-page`, `kube-node-pool-spec`, `load-some-bare-init`) in `text`/`error` fields were corrupted by the app-specific-password sweep; after the patch they survive unmodified while a genuine Apple app password is still masked.

**Real environment tested:** local checkout of this branch on macOS (Node v26.0.0), running the real exported `redactSensitiveFieldValue` / `redactSecrets` from `src/logging/redact.ts` against the pre-fix `main` (`a7f96847ce`) for the before/after contrast — not a mock.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** built a small driver importing the real redactor and ran it with `node --import tsx` against both the pre-fix `main` checkout and this branch:

```
node --import tsx demo.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:** real console output copied from the terminal. BEFORE is the pre-fix `main` checkout; AFTER is this branch.

BEFORE (pre-fix `main` `a7f96847ce`) — console output:
```
[text] "open the help-d…page link and rerun kube-n…spec"
[error] "module load-s…init crashed"
[error] "iCloud rejected abcd-e…mnop"
[apple] "abcd-e…mnop"
```

AFTER (this branch) — console output:
```
[text] "open the help-desk-team-page link and rerun kube-node-pool-spec"
[error] "module load-some-bare-init crashed"
[error] "iCloud rejected abcd-e…mnop"
[apple] "abcd-e…mnop"
```

**Observed result after fix:** the three kebab identifiers are preserved verbatim in `text`/`error` fields and in the persisted-transcript path (`redactSecrets`), while the genuine Apple app password `abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop` is still masked to `abcd-e…mnop` both in the `apple`-named field (unconditional) and in the `error` field where the value contains the `iCloud` context token.

**What was not tested:** end-to-end delivery through a live channel transport (the proof stops at the redactor boundary). The large-value (>32 KiB) chunked-replacement path *is* covered by a dedicated regression test in this PR.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/logging/redact.test.ts` → **113 passed**, including 5 regression cases: kebab survives in `text`/`error`; real Apple password still masked in `apple`/`icloud` fields and when `iCloud`/`app password` context is present in a generic field; and a >32 KiB value where an innocent kebab far from any apple/icloud keyword is **not** masked while a real app-password with nearby context still is.
- `oxlint` clean; `tsgo` clean.

commands.author: Nas01010101

target.title: redact.ts over-masks ordinary aaaa-bbbb-cccc-dddd identifiers as Apple app-specific passwords, corrupting persisted transcript content
target.body:
> Not a security/secret-leak report — this is a **data-integrity** bug (the redactor masks *more* than it should, never less). Filing publicly per that scope; happy to move it if you'd rather.

## Summary

`redactSensitiveFieldValue()` / `redactSecrets()` (`src/logging/redact.ts`, `main` @ `a7f96847ce`) treat any run of four hyphen-separated 4-letter lowercase segments as a suspected Apple app-specific password and mask it. The only false-positive guard is a **10-word allowlist**, which is far too small for the space of 4-letter words, so ordinary kebab-case identifiers, URL slugs, and resource names in **common output fields** are silently corrupted — and the corruption is written into the persisted session transcript.

The pass fires whenever the surrounding field key matches the **very broad** set at `src/logging/redact.ts:94-95`:

```ts
const STRUCTURED_APP_PASSWORD_FIELD_RE =
  /^(?:apple|icloud|app[-_]?specific[-_]?password|appSpecificPassword|application[-_]?password|text|content|message|error|errorMessage|detail|details|reason)$/i;
```

The token pattern and its only guard — a 10-word allowlist — are at `:96` and `:97-108`:

```ts
const APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD_RE = /\b([a-z]{4}-[a-z]{4}-[a-z]{4}-[a-z]{4})\b/g;
const BENIGN_APP_PASSWORD_WORDS = new Set([
  "case",
  "claw",
  "demo",
  "file",
  "main",
  "name",
  "open",
  "path",
  "slug",
  "test",
]);
```

```ts
// :938-939
function looksLikeAppSpecificPassword(candidate: string): boolean {
  return candidate.split("-").every((part) => !BENIGN_APP_PASSWORD_WORDS.has(part.toLowerCase()));
}
```

so via `:1042` (`const shouldRedactAppPassword = redacted !== value || STRUCTURED_APP_PASSWORD_FIELD_RE.test(key);`) it fires on any `text` / `content` / `message` / `error` / `detail` / `reason` value — i.e. ordinary assistant/tool text and error strings.

## Impact

| Field / value | Output | |
|---|---|---|
| `{type:"text", text:"open the help-desk-team-page link and rerun kube-node-pool-spec"}` | `"open the help-d…page link and rerun kube-n…spec"` | ❌ both identifiers corrupted |
| `error: "module load-some-bare-init crashed"` | `"module load-s…init crashed"` | ❌ mangled diagnostics |
| `error: "iCloud rejected abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop"` (a real Apple app password) | `"iCloud rejected abcd-e…mnop"` | ✓ feature works as intended |

The corruption is irreversible and is persisted: `redactSecrets()` feeds the transcript writer at `src/config/sessions/transcript-append.ts:577`. **Trigger:** four consecutive 4-letter lowercase `[a-z]` segments joined by `-`, none in the 10-word allowlist — routinely produced by kebab-case identifiers, k8s resource names, URL slugs, and CSS class lists.

**Severity: low–moderate (data-integrity).** No crash, no secret leak (it over-masks, which is fail-safe for secrets); it silently corrupts legitimate message/log content and poisons the persisted transcript.

## Proposed fix

Tighten the heuristic so it only fires where an Apple app-specific password is actually plausible:

1. **Preferred** — drop the generic field names (`text|content|message|error|errorMessage|detail|details|reason`) from `STRUCTURED_APP_PASSWORD_FIELD_RE`, keeping only `apple|icloud|app-specific-password|application-password`; and don't additionally run `redactAppSpecificPasswords` over arbitrary free text.
2. Or **require an Apple/iCloud context token** within a small window of the candidate before masking — exactly how the Discord-bot-token pattern is gated.

**Sibling-contrast (the house style):** the Discord-token pattern in the same file is context-anchored — it requires the literal `discord` within 40 chars of the token (`:184`, `discord(?:.|\n|\r){0,40}?\b(...)\b`). The app-specific-password pattern has no such anchor, so it matches on free text. The structured-secret field patterns also key off the field *name*, not blind value-matching.

**Provenance:** `git log -S looksLikeAppSpecificPassword -- src/logging/redact.ts` → introduced in `17ceca86` (#79006, "Redact persisted secret-shaped payloads", 2026-05-11); the broad `text|content|message|...` field list was carried through #85196 (2026-06-11). The 10-word allowlist is itself an acknowledgment that false positives were expected — it is simply far too small.

## Reproduction (against the shipped `2026.6.1` build; logic byte-identical on `main`)

```bash
node --input-type=module -e '
import * as m from "/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/redact-B8nIzFk3.js";
console.log(JSON.stringify(m.r({type:"text",text:"open the help-desk-team-page link and rerun kube-node-pool-spec"}).text));
console.log(JSON.stringify(m.i("error","module load-some-bare-init crashed")));
console.log(JSON.stringify(m.i("error","iCloud rejected abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop")));'
```
Real output:
```
"open the help-d…page link and rerun kube-n…spec"
"module load-s…init crashed"
"iCloud rejected abcd-e…mnop"
```

I have a branch ready (option 1 above plus a context anchor mirroring the Discord pattern, with regression cases asserting the kebab-case strings survive while a real Apple password is still masked) and am happy to open the PR. Flagging first since the field-list trim is a judgment call about which fields legitimately carry app passwords.

target.body:
## Summary

Adds remote Codex app-server support for native Codex plugin turns in OpenClaw.

This PR:

- classifies Codex app-server connections as `local-loopback` or `remote`;
- requires bearer-style auth for remote WebSocket app-servers;
- captures Codex initialize runtime identity and uses it in app inventory cache keys and thread binding fingerprints;
- rotates stale remote thread bindings when the remote runtime fingerprint changes;
- supports optional `appServer.remoteWorkspaceRoot` projection so OpenClaw infers the local workspace root, preserves the current cwd suffix, and sends Codex only the final app-server cwd;
- always installs or refreshes configured native Codex plugins through the connected app-server before exposing plugin-owned apps;
- skips the initial app inventory wait when a configured plugin must first be installed;
- refreshes app inventory by plugin-owned app IDs so remote app catalog pagination can stop once required plugin apps are found;
- forces direct dynamic tool loading for remote app-server turns to avoid namespace wrapper schemas rejected by the current remote Codex app-server; and
- updates focused tests and docs for config, activation, app cache keys, thread lifecycle, dynamic tools, startup, targeted app inventory refresh, and the remote app-server config surface.

## Compatibility note

This PR intentionally treats non-loopback Codex app-server WebSocket URLs as remote:

- remote WebSocket app-servers require identity-bearing auth through `appServer.authToken` or an `Authorization` header;
- configured native Codex plugins are always activated with install-and-refresh behavior on the connected app-server;
- operators should only point OpenClaw at remote app-servers trusted to accept OpenClaw-managed plugin installs and app inventory refreshes;
- `remoteWorkspaceRoot` names only the remote app-server workspace root; OpenClaw derives the gateway-side root from the resolved OpenClaw workspace and fails closed if the effective cwd is outside that workspace.

The old `appServer.remoteWorkspace.localRoot` / `remoteRoot` object and the configurable `appServer.remoteMutationPolicy` key were removed from the current config shape. There is no backwards-compat shim in this PR.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Remote OpenClaw gateway profiles can query Google Calendar through a native Codex plugin when the Codex app-server is running on another machine. The live proof covered a remote app-server where Google Calendar started uninstalled and OpenClaw installed it through the install-and-refresh path.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway profile `devbox` connected to remote Codex app-server `wss://kbox8-oai-kevinlin-openclaw-codex.devbox-1.gateway.internal.api.openai.org`.
- Exact setup shape after the current config cleanup:

```sh
scripts/setup-gateway.py --apply --plugins google-calendar --ws-token-file /tmp/codex-ws-auth-token.txt --remote-workspace-root /home/oai/openclaw-workspaces
```

```sh
OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BONJOUR=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 ./openclaw.mjs --profile devbox gateway run --auth none --port 18789 --bind loopback --ws-log compact
```

```sh
./openclaw.mjs --profile devbox agent --session-key agent:codex:remote-gcal-install-refresh-20260618-proof-v3 --message "Use the Google Calendar plugin through the configured remote Codex app-server. What is my Google Calendar schedule tomorrow? For this run, tomorrow is Thursday, June 18, 2026 in America/Los_Angeles. Return the events with times, and say if there are no events." --timeout 900 --json
```

- Evidence from the earlier live install-and-refresh proof:

```text
baseline plugin/read: google-calendar@openai-curated installed=false enabled=false
runId: 502dc4b4-7eb1-42ec-84c5-4b38babe8ed4
status: ok
tool calls: codex_apps.google_calendar.search_events
tool failures: 0
post-run plugin/read: google-calendar@openai-curated installed=true enabled=true
date: 2026-06-18
timezone: America/Los_Angeles
```

Proof artifact:

```text
.mem/main/proofs/demo-38-devbox-remote-google-calendar-install-refresh/proof.md
```

- Observed result after fix: The gateway installed/enabled the remote Google Calendar plugin, completed the Calendar query, and returned the user's Thursday, June 18, 2026 schedule with no Google Calendar tool failures.
- What was not tested: Full local PR gates were not run; focused tests/type/build and the live remote proof were run. The latest commits removed the remote mutation policy and old local-root workspace object after that live proof, so the proof command shown above reflects the cleaned current setup shape.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The setup helper used for the live profile lives in the local OpenAI spec workspace, not this OpenClaw repository PR. The proof artifacts are local under `.mem/main/proofs/...` and are summarized in this PR body.
- Before evidence: Before this follow-up, the install-and-refresh path could block before `plugin/install` because it waited for initial app inventory, then could block on later remote `app/list` pages even after the Google Calendar app appeared.

Earlier remote proof with preinstalled Google Calendar:

```text
runId: 10e57e73-790a-4532-8ff2-98709941e732
status: ok
tool calls: memory_search, codex_apps.google_calendar.get_profile, codex_apps.google_calendar.search_events, codex_apps.google_calendar.batch_read_event
tool failures: 0
event count: 9
date: 2026-06-17
timezone: America/Los_Angeles
proof artifact: .mem/main/proofs/demo-37-devbox-remote-google-calendar/proof.md
```

## Validation

Latest validation on `e0b476d9ec9a6cb9e6cc5ae50752bf4caa664674`:

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-app-cache-key.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts
```

Passed: 4 files, 220 tests.

```sh
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Passed.

```sh
OPENCLAW_BUILD_ALL_NO_PNPM=1 node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup
```

Passed.

```sh
git diff --check
```

Passed in both the OpenClaw checkout and the local OpenAI spec workspace.

```sh
PYTHONPYCACHEPREFIX=/tmp/openclaw-spec-pycache python3 -m py_compile scripts/setup-gateway.py
```

Passed in the local OpenAI spec workspace for the setup helper.

```sh
pnpm docs:list
```

Blocked by internal npm registry auth while pnpm tried to fetch `@opentelemetry/exporter-metrics-otlp-proto-0.219.0.tgz` from `socket-firewall-registry.gateway.unified-30.internal.api.openai.org`: `ERR_PNPM_FETCH_403`, `No authorization header was set for the request`.

Earlier validation on this PR:

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-activation.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-app-cache-key.test.ts
```

Passed: 4 files, 129 tests.

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-diagnostics.test.ts
```

Passed: 1 file, 1 test.

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/schema-normalization-runtime-contract.test.ts
```

Passed: 1 file, 3 tests.

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.binding.test.ts
```

Passed: 1 file, 41 tests.

```sh
uvx showboat verify /Users/kevinlin/code/openclaw/.mem/main/proofs/demo-37-devbox-remote-google-calendar/raw/showboat-summary.md
```

Passed.

target.title: fix(cron): allow empty assistant replies in cron lane (fixes #94224)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Cron jobs that intentionally produce empty assistant replies (silent alert watchers) fail with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response. (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")`. The cron lane has no path to opt into silent empty replies.
- **Root Cause**: The empty-reply guard at `cli-runner.ts:800` only checks `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`, which is never set for cron jobs — it's only computed for direct/group chat semantics. The cron dispatch path sets `lane: "cron"` but never sets `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`.
- **Fix**: Add `params.lane !== "cron"` to the empty-reply guard, so cron jobs producing no output are treated as intentional silent ticks rather than backend failures.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/cli-runner.ts` — add `params.lane !== "cron"` check alongside the existing `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` guard
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - Direct/group chat empty-reply behavior (unchanged)
  - Embedded runner path (already handles empty replies differently via `shouldTreatEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`)
  - Cron job schema, delivery config, or lane management
  - Any other lane's empty-reply behavior

## Root Cause

The throw site at `src/agents/cli-runner.ts:800`:

```typescript
if (!assistantText && !output.didSendViaMessagingTool && params.allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent !== true)
```

The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag is only computed for interactive reply paths (direct/group chats) where it derives from `silentReplyPolicy`. Cron jobs set `lane: "cron"` but never set this flag, so intentionally-silent cron ticks always throw `FailoverError`.

Pre-2026.6.6 this was handled differently; the regression was introduced when the empty-reply guard was tightened.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (#94224)**: Cron jobs that intentionally produce empty assistant replies fail with `FailoverError` instead of succeeding silently; fix exempts the cron lane from the empty-reply guard.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against `src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`

**Evidence before fix**:
```text
Cron jobs with empty replies hit:
FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response.
  reason: "empty_response"
  lane: "cron"
→ job status: error, Gateway log spammed with FailoverError
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  74 passed (74)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
```

**Observed result after fix**: Cron jobs whose `lane === "cron"` no longer throw `FailoverError` when producing an empty assistant reply. All existing cron-related tests continue to pass with no regressions.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end cron job with a claude-cli backend producing empty output; cross-backend testing (only the claude-cli path was verified through existing tests); the `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` interactive reply paths.

**Repro confirmation**: Before the patch, a cron job with `lane: "cron"` and empty output hits the `FailoverError` throw. After the patch, the `params.lane !== "cron"` check exempts cron jobs, allowing them to complete as successful silent ticks.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target test files: `src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`
- Run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`
- Existing cron lane tests continue to pass (74/74)

## Review Findings Addressed

None yet — initial PR submission.

## Merge Risk

- **Risk**: Very low — single-line guard addition in one file
- **Affected code path**: Only the CLI runner empty-reply guard; embedded runner path already handles empty replies differently
- **Test coverage**: 74 existing test cases succeed with no changes needed
- **Rollback**: Trivial — remove the `params.lane !== "cron"` condition

target.title: fix(gateway): show OAuth auth URLs in wizard prompts
target.body:
## Summary

When OAuth setup runs through an RPC wizard client, the gateway can ask for a redirect URL while only printing the authorization URL to gateway stdout. That leaves clients like the Windows companion without the link the user needs to open.

This PR makes redirect-paste OAuth prompts self-contained by including the generated authorization URL directly in the wizard text prompt. Terminal logging is preserved.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: RPC wizard OAuth setup now shows the generated authorization URL in the same text prompt that asks for the redirect URL.
Real environment tested: Windows companion app driving an OpenClaw gateway wizard OAuth setup flow, plus the local Windows OpenClaw worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Opened the Windows companion, started the gateway/provider OAuth wizard flow, verified the wizard prompt displayed the authorization URL before the redirect URL input, opened the URL in a browser, and completed the redirect paste step. Also ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src\plugins\provider-oauth-flow.test.ts extensions\openai\openai-chatgpt-oauth.runtime.test.ts extensions\google\oauth.test.ts extensions\openrouter\oauth.test.ts src\commands\auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts src\wizard\session.test.ts`; `pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`; `pnpm exec oxlint <touched files>`; `git diff --check`.
Evidence after fix: Human verification confirmed the Windows companion wizard showed the OAuth authorization URL inline with the redirect paste prompt. Focused tests also assert authorization URLs are included in prompt messages for the shared OAuth helper, Gemini, OpenAI ChatGPT/Codex, and OpenRouter paths.
Observed result after fix: The Windows companion flow exposed the authorization URL in the wizard prompt and the redirect paste step could be completed. Focused Vitest passed 5 shards / 64 tests; TypeScript core/test and extensions/test checks passed; direct oxlint on touched files passed; `git diff --check` passed.
What was not tested: Full repository `pnpm check`, full repository `pnpm test`, and every provider's live OAuth service.
Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, the gateway printed the authorization URL to `runtime.log` / stdout while the wizard step only asked for a redirect URL.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: OAuth redirect flows treated runtime logs as the user-facing place to show authorization URLs, but RPC wizard clients receive wizard steps rather than gateway stdout.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered local CLI/provider OAuth behavior but not whether RPC-visible prompt messages contained the URL needed to complete manual redirect flows.
- Contributing context (if known): RPC wizard clients need the auth challenge rendered in-band even when the gateway process itself can write the URL to logs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/provider-oauth-flow.test.ts`, `extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth.runtime.test.ts`, `extensions/google/oauth.test.ts`, `extensions/openrouter/oauth.test.ts`, `src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`, `src/wizard/session.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Manual OAuth redirect prompts include the generated authorization URL in the prompt message; auth-presenting RPC prompters use the manual path; CLI/non-auth-presenting paths keep their existing note/log behavior.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers the shared helper, representative provider integrations, and wizard/auth-choice seam without requiring live OAuth credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A; focused coverage was added/updated.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

RPC wizard clients now receive OAuth authorization URLs directly in the text prompt message for redirect-paste flows. Terminal CLI users still see URL logging and the same prompt flow.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
OAuth provider -> gateway runtime.log(url) -> RPC client only sees "paste redirect URL"

After:
OAuth provider -> wizard text message includes url -> RPC client shows url + paste box -> user completes login
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A. The authorization URL was already intentionally logged for user action; this change carries the same non-secret authorization URL in the wizard prompt message. Redirect URLs containing authorization codes remain user input.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm OpenClaw worktree
- Model/provider: Windows companion OAuth wizard flow, plus OpenAI ChatGPT/Codex OAuth, Chutes/shared OAuth helper, Google Gemini CLI OAuth, and OpenRouter OAuth seams via focused tests
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway wizard / provider auth prompts
- Relevant config (redacted): OAuth credentials and generated authorization URLs redacted

### Steps

1. Open the Windows companion against the patched gateway.
2. Start a provider OAuth wizard flow that uses redirect-URL paste.
3. Confirm the wizard text prompt includes the generated authorization URL and the redirect paste instruction.
4. Open the authorization URL in the browser and complete the redirect paste step.

### Expected

- The protocol-visible text step message contains the authorization URL needed to complete OAuth.

### Actual

- The Windows companion showed the authorization URL in the wizard prompt and the redirect paste step completed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Windows companion OAuth wizard prompt showed the generated authorization URL inline with the redirect paste prompt; shared OAuth helper remote and local fallback prompts include the authorization URL; Gemini manual OAuth prompt includes the URL; OpenAI auth-presenting flow collapses copy into the redirect paste prompt; OpenRouter remote OAuth prompt includes the URL.
- Edge cases checked: Existing terminal logging remains; local fallback prompt stays unchanged before an auth URL exists.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repository test suite and every provider's live OAuth service.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Authorization URLs become visible in a wizard prompt message instead of only gateway logs.
  - Mitigation: These URLs were already intentionally shown/logged for users and do not contain the returned authorization code; the secret-bearing redirect remains user input.
- Risk: Prompt messages become longer for OAuth redirect flows.
  - Mitigation: The longer prompt is self-contained and preserves compatibility for clients that render only message text.

AI-assisted: Yes. Implemented with Copilot-assisted sessions and reviewed by separate adversarial compatibility passes before simplifying to the message-only approach.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(cli): avoid false local gateway unreachable on probe timeout
target.body:
## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
target.author: alaleiwang
